item.title: Images of Note: underwater wife
item.description:
The fact these three (presumed) strangers all said "look up in the sky" just seems odd.
it's just horses in here
More Batman TV shows should pay attention to the fact Alfred is ex SAS.
I know the ides were yesterday but HAPPY STABBING CAESAR DAY
What is she watching?
This is not a Cerveza Cristal meme
Priest Kermit, archangelooooooooooooo
CHEESE HIM!
I kinda wich Hailey was a surfer because it would make her so much more interesting.
oh my God
(Yes, JK realy went down the Holocaust denial route with transgender people).
Himbo Elliot. Himbo Elliot
For Chats: http://discord.abbii.gay For Shitposts: http://tumblr.abbii.gay For srs and maybe shitposts: http://mastodon.abbii.gay Also i'm streaming: http://twitch.abbii.gay

item.title: Have a chuckle or 15

item.title: How OP got his first girlfriend at 16 despite being clueless
item.description:
I was 16 and she was 14. We were in the same grade because I never took school seriously and got held back.
I thought she was way out of my league. Didn't even consider she was interested in a dork like me
She tried for like two weeks to seduce me
was thinking on the wat back to my parents house if she was hinting that she wanted me to buy her some clothes. That seemed at the time to be the most likely scenario
And so happy too! I couldn't say yes fast enough
We've talked about a more formal arrangement, but she's divorced and doesn't want to be tied down. Well, she has let me tie her down, but the Cuffs got to come off eventually I have told her I would put a ring on it
item.dc:creator: blankman757

item.title: Dog wants to be shaved too!

item.title: Anibal Julian Nunez, my dad, has passed away
item.description:
Hello Imgur, I've been a quiet lurker on this site daily for nearly ten years, but today I would like to introduce you to somebody very important to me, my dad, Anibal Julian Nunez Rodriguez. I don't know how many people will even read this, and it's difficult to know where to even start. How can an entire person, their impact on the world and what they meant to people be summed up in a single post? It's not possible, but I will do my best to tell you a little bit about him and give an overview of his journey in life. The above image was on his phone. I believe this picture he snapped a photo of was from the 80's.
I don't know much about his early life, but I do know he was born in Asuncion Paraguay on April 6th, 1939, and came from very humble roots. He told me they had dirt floors in their house and the whole neighborhood shared a single radio. Once a week the military would come by to swap out the battery for a fully charged one. Since his father was in the military he said they got extra food too. The above photo is the only known picture to exist of my dad when he was a kid. A dear friend of his, Anibal Cabrera, and his wife Mune, knew my dad from way back. One time I remember them talking about how they stacked paint buckets on top of each other, and that was their dining table. Hearing such stories always made me appreciate the quality of life he managed to give me. He was a trapeze artist in a circus in his youth, and when he was 14 he missed a grab and fell to the ground without a net. He said it was soft dirt, but he still broke a lot of things, and was lucky to be alive. I remember him telling me how years later he would take a bus to get to a telephone to call his girlfriend, and how people in town would call him Jesus because he played him during that reenactment Catholics do. I believe he had the role several times.
Here he is in Rio de Jeneiro with his two best friends, Reinaldo Gomez on the left, Nino Palacios in the center, and him on the right. The three of them formed a band called Trio Parana in 1959. This photo was taken some time between then and the early 1960's. This photo was important to my dad. It marks the start of the journey he and his friends would take in life. He wanted to have it made larger, colorized, and framed nicely. I wanted to do that for him, but AI wasn't doing a good job, and I didn't have the money for the professional human restoration and colorization that this photo deserves at the time. It is something I still want to have done though, it would have meant everything to him. I want to send his two surviving friends copies as well.
The three of them were so young here. He is on the very right.
There are so many photos like this from this era. I will save them for another post though. I need to properly scan them too.
Eventually they got their first record deal. This is his personal copy of their first ever release.
I can't imagine how excited and proud they must have been. From dirt floors to a record deal, talk about moving up in the world. He gave the very same record pictured above to me a few years ago, telling me how important it was to him. So he wanted me to have it. He wasn't the only one in his family with such a story either. One of his brothers, Humberto, became a famous soccer player and a coach after that! Some folks called him Spock since the resemblance is uncanny, and if you look him up on Google images with the key words "Humberto Núñez hercules" you'll see why.
When I was a child I would go through his boxes of trinkets and random things he had collected through his life during his travels. Some of it can be seen above in a snake skin box he had. Everything had a story. From the keys to a city a mayor gave him, turquoise he got at a mine he visited, to the panda a woman made for him long before I was born. Everything had a story, I just wish I could remember them. Everything is in the same condition as it was decades ago, I have always taken good care of things. When my younger self would ask him if I could have this or that he would always say yes without hesitation, and he would tell me that it would be mine one day anyway. When you're a kid you can't even imagine that day ever coming, even now it doesn't seem real. I have a house full of stuff that still feels like it is his.
Their group gained a harpist, Lorenzo Gonzalez, and Trio Parana became Los Paranas. Their popularity was booming and in 1969 they met an American soldier named Jose Mauricio Paiz. He loved their music and set them up to get their visas sponsored to bring them to the United States. My dad always spoke very highly of Mr. Paiz, and when I finally met him for the first time it was like meeting a celebrity I heard stories about all my life. I have since met him on several occasions now and he is a wonderful and wholesome human being. My grandmother, my dad's mom, called him Senior Mr. Paiz. The group teased her about that at first, but eventually it stuck and Senior Mr. Paiz became a nickname. I never got to meet my grandmother unfortunately, my dad was past middle aged when I was born, but all the stories I have heard about her make me wish I had. I have some long recordings of Mr. Paiz and the group just sitting around telling stories between playing music. There are so many things like this I need to go through and put online. I want to preserve as much of their history as I can. Side note, somebody wrote a biography of Mr. Paiz's life called Mauricio José Paiz: Military Heroes of Puerto de Luna.
This is Los Paranas playing on Siempre En Domingo with a good friend of theirs, Marco Antonio Muniz. The story I recall is the above photo was taken the day before a big earthquake in Mexico city that claimed the life of another friend of theirs. That is one thing about my dad that could always be counted on, he always had a story to tell from his life, travels, and involvement in the music industry. It seemed like whenever there was a song on the radio, or show on TV he would be reminded of something related and start off on one of his stories. All of them were interesting to me, and it never got old even if I had heard it before. Some are so well ingrained in my memory, but there are so many more I can barely remember now. I have never known anyone who so consistently had so many interesting things to recount. A lot of these stories even had photographs he could dig out to go along with them.
Like this one for example. The famous soccer player, Pele, was also a good friend of theirs and stayed with them for a long time. I think this was in New Jersey.
I have probably hundreds of photos to go through, but to sum up my dad's music career, it went well. He had been all around the world, met and befriended countless famous, influential, and interesting people, and did so many things during his decades long music career. Most of this before I was even born. Although for the record, I do have older siblings. For the first maybe two decades of my life I didn't even think much of it, I only had the vaguest memories of it from very early childhood. It just seemed so normal for me to hear my dad tell stories all of the time. All I knew was he was my dad, and he could sing and play the guitar. It was just something there in the background on top of all the usual growing up and life stuff. It wasn't until I started to properly mature as a person that I started to take more of an interest and connect with him. The older I got, the more I felt he and I understood each other, and the more he wanted to share with me. Looking back I now understand that he was feeling the wear of time and was opening up more in his own way. He would show me folders of pictures, different items of clothing, explain this and that, and give me things to keep. Things he very clearly had sentimental attachment to. These last few years especially he kept giving me more and more. He would approach me wanting me to take things, or to keep something in my room, always with a story attached. I now know he was preparing for me to wear his crown one day, and wanted me to understand what everything meant to him. I'm sorry I don't remember all of the stories, dad, but I will take good care of everything all the same. I do have a few on video and several written down at least, and they are worth everything to me. Sometimes I would ask him details afterwards just so I could make sure what I was about to write was as detailed as possible. The thing that really gets me is I was actively working towards recording his stories regularly because I knew he wouldn't be around forever, but he passed so suddenly. He had such an interesting life and so many stories in him to tell, it seems so very unfair. I know others can fill in a lot of the gaps, but I wanted him to be the one telling me. I wanted to save that. I am sure those of you reading this have loved ones with stories to tell, do yourselves a favor and record as many of them as possible whenever the moment presents itself. Ask them all the things you are curious about, don't let their stories go untold.
In case anyone reading this was wondering, this is the sound of the group he was part of, Los Paranas. Sound on and volume up very much recommended! I clipped it here since my dad starts to sing a solo section starting at 0:48. He had such powerful voice and was said to be the romantic voice of the group. I have heard women had quite literally threw their panties at him back in the day, ha! I believe it. His version of Bésame Mucho in particular was famously well praised, and having heard it myself I can't argue. I have a lot of things to sort through at the moment, but will put that online eventually. The song clipped above is called "Los Paranas - Canto a las Américas". It is on youtube as well as Spotify if anyone wanted to listen to the full thing. They have 28 albums total but only a small selection is online. There's a new official channel but nothing's on it yet. I have most of the group's professional master tape reels from RCA in my possession in perfect condition, as well as a era appropriate professional studio equipment that has been well maintained. The plan was to get everything digitized, remastered, and shared with the modern world. He was going to show me how to use the old equipment, while I would help him with the new, but now that is all on me. I know I can figure it out, and there are probably plenty of tutorials out there, I just wish he could have shown me more of it himself. The guitar he has on the album cover above is a Kramer 450B Bass Natural 1978 (s275) I believe. A man from Kramer took him to lunch with Farrah Fawcett and Kris Kristofferson at a Hilton and gave him that guitar as a gift to promote the company. Sadly it was stolen years later at the Shangri-La in Oklahoma. He loved that guitar and I always wanted to surprise him with another one some day. I still plan to get one when I can afford it. It will be his guitar, I'll just play it for him.
During his travels with his group one thing I am told he famously loved to do was cook and prepare food. After retiring from his music career in 1989, my dad went on to pursue his other calling and became a chef for the following 20 years. The above photo is from 2005. He genuinely enjoyed cooking and working in that industry. He had so many stories of his friends in the kitchen, interesting or famous guests he would meet, and what he did that day. Banquets at the Radisson hotel always seemed particularly interesting, he learned to carve quite fancy ice sculptures for those. I wish I had photos, but at least got to see some in person. One of the stories I remember from those days was of the employees talking about something that happened during World War 2, and he mentioned how he remembered hearing about that event on the radio at the time. They were shocked and asked him, "just how old are you?", and my dad crossed his arms, stood proud in his chef uniform, and responded with "who do you think prepared The Last Supper?". I loved his sense of humor. Another time he was in the hotel kitchen singing "I Left My Heart in San Francisco", but replaced "heart", with "horse" to mess with a new employee, who tried to correct him, and dad would respond that he is pretty sure it is horse. The rest of the employees knew the play and backed up the story. This was before everyone had the internet in their pocket, so they all got to watch the new guy's descent into insanity for the rest of the day as he got Mandela effected and tried to find somebody who remembered the version he knew. During all his years cooking he perfected an amazing seasoning that was widely praised. We always wanted to get that on the market but never got around to it properly. I have the recipe and need to figure out how to go about doing that soon. There was also a man at the Radisson Hotel in San Antonio, Texas, who simply went by Buddy. He worked in the kitchen too and the two of them were best friends during all his years there. I only met him a few times, and I can't seem to find his contact information, but if anyone might know who that is please reach out in DMs.
After retiring he spent much of his time around the house. He loved to relax in his chair drinking coffee from his usual thermos while watching TV, and would often have the family dogs up there with him like the photo above. He absolutely loved animals, and they loved him. Taking care of them all day kept him busy but he didn't mind. They made him happy. When not doing that he liked to do things like mess with his audio equipment and listen to his group from the old days along with other music he liked. Sometimes there would be a big project like making a new double gate for the side yard and would completely absorb himself in that. He went all out curving the wood and creating physical patterns on the doors, making it real fancy-like! He also liked to make ornate, aged looking picture frames, along with many other projects throughout the years. When it came time for food he was always on top of that too, and he'd ask if you were hungry and wanted something to eat if you woke up or arrived when it wasn't typical meal time, even if he'd never met you before. This includes folks who came by to trim trees or do some kind of work like that. For many years there were gatherings of family and close friends on holidays, sometimes those friends would bring their own family or friends. He loved it not only because of the company, but because he got to cook a lot of food like he used to during his time as a chef. No matter the size of the get together there was always too much food it seemed. Not that anyone minded taking home some of the extra.
He also loved to visit thrift stores on weekend outings, sometimes pawn shops too. The above photo was taken during his birthday outing in 2017. I'd always get the front seat when going along to such events, and he'd be sitting there in the back. Going to a diner here named Jim's was typical, he'd order a frontier burger, and when the waitress asked what he wanted to drink, he'd sometimes respond, "scotch and water", then order some coffee after a little laugh. He also loved that shirt for those outings (it's beige, but the lighting makes it seem greenish), it was from some member's only club in New Jersey I believe. Despite having it for decades it still looked nearly new. COVID understandably really put a damper on outings, but I think that was fairly typical for older generations.
There were some real nice gatherings spread out over the years though, the above was taken at the Los Paranas 50th anniversary party. From left to right: Mr. Paiz, Nino Palacios, the late Lorenzo Gonzales (the group's first harpist), Reinaldo Gomez, and dad. The picture certainly doesn't show it, but the place was packed with a whole bunch of people.
From the same event with more friends. He always had such a good time at these gatherings.
This is from his 84th birthday from April of 2023. His best friend, Reinaldo, hosted a nice party for him at his place with a bunch of their dear old friends. From how my dad described everything and the glow he had about him while doing so, I could tell that he had a lovely time and really enjoyed himself. More than had in a really long time. He deserved it so much. I had wanted to attend but was unfortunately unable to at the time, I really wish I could have now, but I am glad he had such a good time regardless. He looks so happy here, it both makes me smile and breaks my heart. Nobody knew it would be his last birthday, I'm glad he had fun.
Group photo with some of his dearest friends. Both this and the above video were taken with his phone. I really want to get photos and videos from other people at that party some day soon.
This is one of the silly selfies I found on his phone. He looks so happy in every single one, and it makes me both smile and teary eyed to see them now. Over the past year he had been trying to learn how to use a modern smartphone, it's daily life for most of us, but you have to remember that he was born in 1939. It took a lot of getting used to, and a lot of question asking, but he was getting the hang of it. I was so happy to see him learning and getting enjoyment out of it. He was learning whatsapp and contacting old friends and family regularly without having to pay international calling fees, and getting to talk to them daily even! He loved that and got back into regular contact with people again, it made him feel like part of the world once more and brought so much enjoyment back into his life. I remember the day his sister in Paraguay, Estella, took a picture she found of their father in his military uniform and sent it over whatsapp. My dad hadn't seen so much as a photo of him in decades and when he saw that, he got teary eyed and said "papa" in a trembling voice, kissing his phone repeatedly. I could not help feeling like I wanted to cry myself, I'd never seen that kind of reaction from him in my entire life. He came to me with so many questions, or thought he messed something up and wanted help trying to fix it. I was so glad that he was trying to learn, so I always made the time to answer and help him figure it out. I never wanted him to feel like he was being annoying or any kind of burden, not even once. I am proud to say that I always made the time for him and so thankful for that now, and I know he was too. He was learning how to use youtube, and loved to watch videos showing how things were made, guitar channels, and looking up other South American bands with people he knew. I know seeing people scroll through a phone brings about the mental image of somebody mindlessly rotting their brain consuming content, but to him? It was pure amazement and opened a new world. He was full of genuine wonder again and it was a joy to see. He came to me to show me so many things he found online, and I would patiently watch and try to show interest. I have his youtube history saved and want to go through it sometime, especially to find things I remember him being particularly excited about. I am really glad we got him to try that phone. Even though he didn't even have it a full year before he passed it brought such enrichment to his life. I can only imagine how much more joy learning modern technology would have brought him if he got to have more time in this world. During this past year, I think for the first time in my own life I truly understood the pure joy of simply enjoying seeing somebody you love being truly happy, and that being all you need. I just wanted him to be happy in his twilight years, that was all.
This was a typical scene you'd see for half the day, him sitting in that chair with a dog on his lap. Josephine there was attached to him like his shadow, and I know she really misses her daddy right now. I wish I could explain to her, but I think she knows. This video was taken right before he went on a week long trip to Dallas with his friend Reinaldo to meet up with Nino and some of their other friends, back in August of 2023.
None of them knew this would be the last time they'd all be together again, but oh my gosh he had such a good time! They all did. Those three young men standing on the rocks in Rio at the start of this post, now 64 years later, got to have one last trip with the boys like the good 'old days! I'm so glad he got to go, and that he had such a great time. I miss him terribly but he had some of the most fun he's had in ages during his last year in this world, with so many pictures of him smiling and having a good time. From left to right, Reinaldo, Nino, their friend Chiquito, and dad. I don't know Chiquito's actual name, but my dad always spoke very highly of him. I met him a few times too and he was good company.
He showed me the pictures and videos he took from that trip, and really seemed to like this mall in Dallas especially.
Another photo from his phone, this is the the Christmas tree he set up for Christmas 2023. I don't think anyone else was really cared about or was feeling the holiday that year, I know I wasn't. But he clearly wanted a Christmas tree, so he dug it out of storage, put it together, and decorated it by himself. I'm glad he did, it made me feel something for the season when I saw it. I told him something praising along the lines of he did a great job. The tree stayed up well into the new year, and I think it will always be my favorite Christmas tree from now on in my memory. Right now I don't know how I can bring myself to set up a tree or celebrate anything anymore knowing he's no longer around. I'm sure this is common. When my brother died when I was younger, in December no less, I recall feeling the same thing, but I guess time has a way of resolving those feelings. We'll see.
On February 7th he made a huge batch of empanadas, the above photo is the yet to be mixed filling before being put in the dough shells. I have a several minute long video of him talking to me while making them that I recorded on that day as well. I had always meant to document the process on video, and now I am so glad that I did. His empanadas were always delicious and one of my absolute favorite foods ever since I was little. There are still a bunch in the freezer waiting to be deep fried, but I almost don't want to do it because they will be the last ever empanadas that he made. He made them for me though, I know he'd want me to enjoy them, I just can't yet.
Around February 17th or 18th I think, he had some kind of cough. Nobody thought much of it. My mom had a cough before that but it didn't seem too last too long, then my dad and I had a sore throat and cough after that at the same time. Mine wasn't too bad and was only for maybe three days, just coughing on occasion when I was speaking. It wasn't a big deal. His cough kept progressing and getting worse though. Eventually he started acting like he wasn't quite himself. He didn't want to watch TV and barely ate. He tried to clean the floor with cooking spray, and we knew something was very, very wrong. I urged to have him go to the hospital, and on February 22nd the EMS came to get him. I recall him sitting in his chair as the paramedics checked him out, and him saying "I hate to be sick" right before he got up and they helped him walk out of the house and into the ambulance. He insisted on walking himself. He stayed the night, and so much was going on nobody was able to go see him the next day. His doctor was talked to and they said he was COVID positive. His strange behavior was suspected to be from low oxygen levels, so they put him on a bi-pap mask to help get him breathing right. They said he was doing well now, and the same the next day. We figured he'd be home again soon. He was vaccinated and hadn't gone out much for a while. My mom on the other hand went places all of the time, never wearing a mask. She also didn't want to enforce masks for her piano students as not to cause any conflict. She didn't take it seriously because "nobody else is", despite my reprimanding her. I have always worn my mask when out places since COVID began, I just wish other people cared too. Since he went to the hospital I had barely been able to sleep or even take care of myself, and would call asking how he was doing, and getting updates on his oxygen levels. On the 25th we went to visit him, he had a bipap mask on so it was difficult to understand what he was saying, but I wanted to see him and for him to know how much I cared about him and how he was doing. The above photo is from that day, he is just out of frame since I know that is not how he would to be seen. The nurse in charge of his room was called several times during the night, and made it sound like he was doing better. We went again the next day and now he was on an oxymizer (under nose air tube), which was a lot more comfortable for him and made conversation easier. We talked for a good while, and I let him know how much I cared. That morning on the 26th I took a video of his dog, and pictures around the house since I thought he'd like to see his home again. When showing those to him he smiled and said "we have a nice home". I could tell it really cheered him up and am so glad he got to see his home one last time, even if only on a phone screen. There was also a really nice guitar he had been trying to pay off for months, not for himself, but so I could have it. He wanted it for me even though I am not very good yet. My sister paid off the remaining balance and my dad got to see it a picture of it safe in my room. I know that had to have made him happy too. Of course I couldn't stay at the hospital forever and had to leave eventually. I told him how much I love him, and that he has to eat and get better so he can come home. I remember him looking up at me with his blue eyes and saying "I'll come home", but there was something about how he was looking at me, and the tone in his voice that made me feel like he was only saying it to try and make me feel better. I don't think he believed his own words. That memory will stay with me forever. I didn't want to leave, but he was genuinely looking and acting like he was feeling better than the day before, so I tried to focus on that to have some peace of mind. I was so completely exhausted from lack of sleep, sore and needed to wash my hair and do a lot of much needed self care that I just had to stay home the next day. When the hospital was called he was resting, but the nurse said how responsive he was, how his oxygen levels were up due to adding nitrous, and the overall impression was that he was doing better. So I felt comfortable taking an off day, and I know he wouldn't want me to wear myself out for his sake.
Ever since a young age I've drank yerba mate. My dad introduced it to me as "Paraguayan tea", I did not know, or at least remember its proper name until maybe my late teens. Even though I could make it myself, he liked to make home made tea bags and even prepare it for me as often as he could. The above is the last remaining amount that he made for me, 10 bags. I remember crying with worry every time I got home from the hospital, and all through the night. I remember opening the jar above and looking inside, knowing that those 10 bags might be the last ones my dad would ever have prepared for me. The above photo was taken in the 26th of February. The 27th was the day I stayed home mentioned above. When calling in the very early morning hours of the 28th they said he wasn't eating much, and it was asked if it would be okay to bring some of his favorite soft foods. He loved strawberry applesauce, and having milk and a banana before bed, so we were going to start with that. They said we could bring an ice chest and they would keep it topped off to keep his personal food store cold. They said he also wanted a little fan, so buying one while at the store to take to him was on the list as well. He seemed to be doing okay like the day before, so there wasn't too much worry, but I was concerned that they had to increase the amount of nitrous they were giving him. The doctor called later and said that they had to intubate him, and his heart stopped while doing so, so they had to do CPR to revive him, twice. The compressions broke several of his ribs and one of his lungs collapsed so they had to put in a chest tube too. He was now officially on a ventilator. Myself, my mom and my sister went to the hospital together some time in the afternoon. We were given a run down by a doctor there who made it sound like it was possible for him to recover. He said about everything else was fine, it was just his lungs that were the problem. Later a nurse named Mia came by, she was one who helped revive him earlier. She laid things out very bluntly without sugar coating anything like everyone else had She told the facts exactly how they were, and it was as bad as I expected things were going, but I was glad to finally get direct answers. I think my mom and sister may have thought she was kind of heartless or mean, but I thanked her because it is exactly what I needed to know. I didn't want false hope or lies, I needed to know reality and couldn't have been more thankful for her blunt honesty. If not for her I wouldn't have had the solid conviction that I needed for what was to follow. I couldn't help but be angry at the whole situation. He had such an interesting life, he had done so many things, he still had so much to live for, and we still had so many plans. He shouldn't have been dying in a hospital room because stupid people didn't care about considering others. It's only the sick and the old they say, but their "only" is somebody's everything. I absolutely hate this country. This was entirely preventable and wasn't fair.
It was clear he wasn't going to get any better. He wasn't ever going to wake up again and he was only being kept alive for the sake of technically being alive. That isn't what he would have wanted, it's not what anyone wanted. I couldn't allow that to continue. The decision on what to do fell solely on me, everything in my life always seemed to, but this was the worst yet. I signed the papers and they were to put him on what they called comfort care, which meant they were going to take him off the ventilator and give him increasing amounts of morphine and I think other things so he would hopefully be as comfortable as possible. It would take a while for the orders to go through, so they suggested we go to the cafeteria in the meantime. So we did. We had some of the grilled salmon, which wasn't anywhere near as good as my dad made. My sister had a salad too, and I think so did my mom. It doesn't really matter. After eating and just trying to de-stress for a while, we all headed back up. I think shortly after they were ready to proceed, so we all stepped out into the hall as they pulled the curtain to remove his air tube, and proceeded to ease him into comfort care. I had signed the order to not revive him. It was the worst thing I've ever had to sign in my life, especially since the rest of my family completely put the weight on me alone. The three of us stayed in the room and talked to him for maybe an hour and a half, maybe two. I do not really remember how long it was. Eventually my sister called her husband to come pick her up, and my mom went home so she could take care of her new dogs. I told them I was staying, so I stayed. He had been there for me as best he could for my entire life, so I wanted to be there for him. I couldn't let him die alone. A very kind young nurse named Madison brought a recliner with a blanket and pillows for me after a few hours, and she checked in many times to both give my dad more morphine, and see what she could do for me. She said most of the time she doesn't really get to know patients when they end up there, but that she was glad she had the opportunity to get to know my dad a little. I talked about him with some of the staff there that night when they came by, and when I showed them just who he was, and they heard his singing voice their eyes widened and they were completely floored learning that was the dying old man in their care this whole time. One nurse commented how pleasant and humble he was, saying he never mentioned any of that. That sounds like him. The staff made it sound like he wouldn't last long without assisted breathing, but he lasted over seven hours. I talked to him nearly the entire time, only sleeping briefly twice. I told him everything I could think to tell, held his hand, and stroked his head. I genuinely ran out of things to say, things to apologize for, and told him I loved him and how important he was, and how much I needed him I don't know how many times. I don't know if he could even hear me, but I hoped he could. Even if he couldn't... he knew all of that anyway. I know there was nothing to forgive, and that he knew how I felt. Anything I could tell him, he already knew. He passed away on February 29th at 5:09 AM. After I had some time with him I called my mom to let her know, then his best friend, Reinaldo at 5:18AM. He was sick himself (just a bad flu), so his wife, Gina, did most of the talking. The photo above was taken while I waited outside the room while the nurses cleaned up my dad's body and prepped him for viewing. After they were done I went back in the room and talked to him some more, just in case any part of him was still around. They say hearing is the last thing to go, so I kept talking while I waited for my mom to arrive so she could say her own goodbyes.
It was strange seeing my dad's body laying there, I was not bothered by the sight as much as I thought I would be. Odd as it may seem, the bodies of those who have passed do not bother me a whole lot, even when they are loved ones. What use is a body if the person you love is no longer in there? It barely even seems like them anymore. What got to me, and still continues to is knowing he wouldn't be around anymore. Alive or not though, that was my dad, that was the man I had known all my life and now he was gone. I knew he wasn't in there anymore, but I didn't want to leave him, but I also I couldn't stay forever. I knew the body laying on the bed would be less and less him the longer I stayed, and that is not how I wanted to remember him, and I know it is not how he would want to be remembered. The picture above was taken with him laying just out of frame below. I wouldn't dare take a photo of him as he was then, so this is all there is of the last time I would see my dad in this world. The orange bag contains his clothes and personal items, which are now at my home. I told him I loved him, and that I would make him proud. That I would digitize all of his music and let the modern world know who he was, that I would learn to play that guitar he wanted me to have so badly, and I am sure I said other things I can no longer recall. I held his hand one last time, and said goodbye.
Most of the world doesn't know who was lost that day, but I will be dedicating my life to trying to make sure as many people possible do. I have to preserve his legacy. He was one of the single most interesting and talented people I'd ever known or even heard about, and he had an incredibly well lived and accomplished life. He came from dirt floors and rose to fame, lived through World War 2, and had been all over the world. He saw it all, and did it all several times over, and fluently spoke five languages on top of that. He lived a life more full than most can even dream of, but most importantly, above everything else, he was my dad. It still doesn't seem real that I'll never see him again.
Life is too short, cherish the time you have with those you love. Let them know what you want them to know, and do the things with them you want to do as soon as possible, because tomorrow isn't guaranteed. Moments in time with those you love are the most precious things that will ever be. Even the mundane of daily life you take for granted will one day become a precious memory you'd give anything to experience again. I love you, dad.
item.dc:creator: LosParanas

item.dc:creator: zoraniko

item.title: I literally don't know what to do with my time
item.description: Fp edit? With this many notes? Thanks all for the really amazing advice. I’ve been following and will sleep a lot better tonight. This has all been a really stressful few years and I hoping some void shouting and internet stranger advice would help and it totally did and I’m beyond grateful. — Edit2: I'm taking everything y'all have said seriously. I just got done talking to the police, they were really receptive and took me seriously too! Now I'm contacting the security at my work, even though I don't go in until Monday. I have confirmation that he's in Texas at his mom's house and she'll tell me if he leaves-it would take him 13-16 hours to get here if he did. I'm calling up some friends to help me box and move what he left behind once the early morning has gone. At the same time, I'm trying to figure out how to say this to my landlord without feeling super pathetic and problematic. Thank you all so much, this has been incredibly hard and y'all helped me not feel alone or scared. Spent most of the day pacing around, talking to my grandma, my sister, and my dad, and my ex's? (feels weird to say that) mom. He was throwing shit again today and threw a recliner out the door, threw food and spices on the floor and kitchen appliances off the counters...I confronted him and said I was done with this BS. This is the fourth time he's started throwing stuff. The first time he actually broke a few hundred dollars worth of my computer stuff, specifically a monitor and chair. He gathered up his stuff and I thought he left to go pout in the garage until I would come and beg for him to come back inside. Got a call from his mom asking why he would be asking for gas money and I said that I wasn't really sure, idk where he'd be going...well it turns out he was on his way to her house (she's not even there). I feel so free...but I have no fucking clue what to do. As long as I've been an adult I've been in a relationship and have lived with someone, this is my first time being really alone. It feels so weird! I've been afraid all day thinking he'll come inside any minute to keep yelling at me and yet didn't get the courage to go see if he was actually still in the garage until his mom asked that question. He should make it to her house in a couple of hours from now. Last night before I went to bed when he started an argument, he locked me in the room because he said if he had access to me he would wind up throwing hands, which is his very nice way of saying he'll beat the shit out of me. I am 70lbs on a good day, y'all, so I took that threat a little seriously. So when he was throwing stuff again this morning, I was not feeling very safe. As much as there is to all of it, I'm so relieved I don't have to wake up tomorrow and keep cleaning the same messes, figuratively and literally. It's going to be really weird learning how to sleep alone. Otherwise...I'm surprisingly happy and feeling somewhat surreal. Idk. I need to say this in the world. I've come out on another account before about him abusing me and y'all were really supportive of helping me get out. That encouragement really helped me put my foot down and stand up for myself, but I felt ashamed that I was still with him and he was still doing all the stuff I came out about in that post. I dont have that account or post anymore (was safer for me to abandon it since I was still with him..) And it's wild I got to get out of it this easily like I didn't have to use the escape plans I worked on developing or even wait for the date I was waiting for...all my friends are asleep or busy right now . Thanks for letting me put this out in the world and feel less lonely for a few minutes. :) Y'all are the best
item.dc:creator: clumsycalico

item.title: Meanwhile in Turkey
item.description: In Turkey, a container ship accidentally toppled three giant cranes The incident took place at the Turkish international port of Kocaeli. Fortunately, there were no reported casualties.
